page_content: ENGL455 The 18th-Century British Novel (3 Credits) Explore the beginnings of the novel in the late seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. Whether set in exotic locales, rural English homes, or the metropolis of London, novels represented reputedly "ordinary" people--often adolescents--and their daily lives. But what constituted a novel and how it influenced readers was debated throughout the century, and even its name ("novel," "romance," "history," "moral tale") was fluid. The novels we will consider are experimental, adventurous, and metaﬁctional, and they will provoke us to evaluate what we expect of the genre, how it functions, and its aesthetic and cultural stakes. Authors may include Aphra Behn, Daniel Defoe, Eliza Haywood, Samuel Richardson, Henry Fielding, Laurence Sterne, Frances Burney, and Sarah Scott. Prerequisite: Two English courses beyond Fundamental Studies; or permission of ARHU-English Department.
